From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Medieval Catholic hymn to Mary, mother of Jesus

For the university, see [Salve Regina University](/wiki/Salve_Regina_University "Salve Regina University").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Raphael_Madonna_dell_Granduca.jpg/250px-Raphael_Madonna_dell_Granduca.jpg)](/wiki/File:Raphael_Madonna_dell_Granduca.jpg)

[Madonna](/wiki/Madonna_(art) "Madonna (art)") by [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael"), an example of [Marian art](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Marian_art "Roman Catholic Marian art")



Salve Regina attributed to [Hermann von Reichenau](/wiki/Hermann_von_Reichenau "Hermann von Reichenau") (1013–1054), sung by Les Petits Chanteurs de Passy. (Gregorian notation below.)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Salve_Regina.png/250px-Salve_Regina.png)](/wiki/File:Salve_Regina.png)

The Salve Regina in solemn tone, [neume](/wiki/Neume "Neume") notation, used in [Gregorian chant](/wiki/Gregorian_chant "Gregorian chant")

The "**Salve Regina**" ([/ˌsælveɪ rəˈdʒiːnə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SAL-vay rə-JEE-nə*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), [Ecclesiastical Latin](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Latin_language "Ecclesiastical Latin language"): [[ˈsalve reˈdʒina]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Latin "Help:IPA/Latin"); meaning "Hail Queen"), also known as the "**Hail Holy Queen**", is a [Marian hymn](/wiki/Marian_hymn "Marian hymn") and one of four [Marian antiphons](/wiki/Marian_antiphons "Marian antiphons") sung at different seasons within the [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") [liturgical calendar](/wiki/Liturgical_calendar "Liturgical calendar") of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"). The Salve Regina is traditionally sung at [Compline](/wiki/Compline "Compline") in the time from the Saturday before [Trinity Sunday](/wiki/Trinity_Sunday "Trinity Sunday") until the Friday before the first Sunday of [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent"). The *Hail Holy Queen* is also the final prayer of the [Rosary](/wiki/Rosary "Rosary").

The work was composed during the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") and originally appeared in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), the prevalent language of Western Christianity until modern times. Though traditionally ascribed to the eleventh-century German monk [Hermann of Reichenau](/wiki/Hermann_of_Reichenau "Hermann of Reichenau"), as well as to [Bernard of Clairvaux](/wiki/Bernard_of_Clairvaux "Bernard of Clairvaux"), [Peter of Compostela](/wiki/Peter_of_Compostela "Peter of Compostela") and [Adhemar of Le Puy](/wiki/Adhemar_of_Le_Puy "Adhemar of Le Puy"), among others,[[1]](#cite_note-DaytonAntiphons-1) it is regarded as anonymous by most musicologists.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Traditionally it has been sung in Latin, though many translations exist. These are often used as spoken prayers.

## Background and history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Salve_Regina&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background and history")]

Marian antiphons have been sung, since the thirteenth century, at the close of [Compline](/wiki/Compline "Compline"), the last Office of the day. [Peter Canisius](/wiki/Peter_Canisius "Peter Canisius") (d. 1597) noted that one praises God in Mary when one turns to her in song.[[3]](#cite_note-Dayton-3) Liturgically, the Salve Regina is the best known of four prescribed [Marian Anthems](/wiki/Marian_Anthems "Marian Anthems") recited after Compline, and, in some uses, after [Lauds](/wiki/Lauds "Lauds") or other Hours.[[4]](#cite_note-frisk-4) Its use after Compline is likely traceable to the monastic practice of intoning it in chapel and chanting it on the way to sleeping quarters.[[5]](#cite_note-carol-5)

It was set down in largely its current form at the Abbey of [Cluny](/wiki/Cluny "Cluny") in the 12th century, where it was used as a processional hymn on Marian feasts. The Cistercians chanted the Salve Regina daily from 1218.[[4]](#cite_note-frisk-4) It was popular at medieval universities as an evening song, and according to Juniper Carol, it came to be part of the ritual for the blessing of a ship.[[5]](#cite_note-carol-5) While the anthem figured largely in liturgical and in general popular Catholic devotion, it was especially dear to sailors.[[6]](#cite_note-Henry-6)

In the 13th century, in either the 1220s or 1230s, the [Dominican friars](/wiki/Dominican_Order "Dominican Order") in Bologna adopted the nightly Salve procession at Compline, and this practice soon spread throughout their entire order. Because the Dominicans were so diffuse in Europe even in their early years, the popularity of the singing and procession is largely attributed to them. Pope Gregory IX, on the advice of the Dominican St. [Raymond of Penyafort](/wiki/Raymond_of_Penyafort "Raymond of Penyafort"), instituted the practice for the diocese of Rome on Fridays at Compline, and many dioceses and religious orders throughout Europe are recorded as adopting the practice in some form or another throughout the 13th century.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In the 18th century, the Salve Regina served as the outline for the classic [Roman Catholic Mariology](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Mariology "Roman Catholic Mariology") book *[The Glories of Mary](/wiki/The_Glories_of_Mary "The Glories of Mary")* by [Alphonsus Liguori](/wiki/Alphonsus_Liguori "Alphonsus Liguori"). In the first part of the book Alphonsus, a [Doctor of the Church](/wiki/Doctor_of_the_Church "Doctor of the Church"), discusses the Salve Regina and explains how God gave Mary to mankind as the "Gate of Heaven".[[8]](#cite_note-8)

It was added to the [series of prayers](/wiki/Leonine_Prayers "Leonine Prayers") said at the end of [Low Mass](/wiki/Low_Mass "Low Mass") by [Pope Leo XIII](/wiki/Pope_Leo_XIII "Pope Leo XIII").[[6]](#cite_note-Henry-6)

The Salve Regina is traditionally sung at the end of a priest's funeral Mass. It is typically sung immediately after the Dismissal.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

As a prayer, it is commonly said at the end of the [rosary](/wiki/Rosary "Rosary").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Latin text

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Salve_Regina&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Latin text")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Giovanni_Battista_Tiepolo_076.jpg/250px-Giovanni_Battista_Tiepolo_076.jpg)](/wiki/File:Giovanni_Battista_Tiepolo_076.jpg)

[Madonna](/wiki/Madonna_(art) "Madonna (art)") by [Tiepolo](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Tiepolo "Giovanni Battista Tiepolo"), 1760

> *Salve, Regina, Mater misericordiæ,  
> vita, dulcedo, et spes nostra, salve  
> Ad te clamamus exsules filii Hevæ  
> Ad te suspiramus, gementes et flentes  
> in hac lacrimarum valle.*  
>   
> *Eia, ergo, advocata nostra, illos tuos  
> misericordes oculos ad nos converte;  
> Et Iesum, benedictum fructum ventris tui,  
> nobis post hoc exsilium ostende.  
> O clemens, O pia, O dulcis Virgo Maria.*

In some cases, the following [versicle](/wiki/Versicle "Versicle") and [collect](/wiki/Collect "Collect") are added:[[11]](#cite_note-11)

> *℣ Ora pro nobis, sancta Dei Genitrix,  
> ℟ Ut digni efficiamur promissionibus Christi.  
> [Oremus](/wiki/Oremus "Oremus").  
> Omnipotens sempiterne Deus, qui gloriosæ Virginis Matris Mariæ corpus et animam, ut dignum Filii tui habitaculum effici mereretur, Spiritu Sancto cooperante præparasti: da, ut cuius commemoratione lætamur; eius pia intercessione, ab instantibus malis, et a morte perpetua liberemur. Per eundem Christum Dominum nostrum.  
> ℟ Amen.*

The current text is almost exactly that of the original; however, the word "Mater" in the first line seems to have been added in the 16th century, and "Virgo" in the final line in the 13th.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Variations exist among most translations.

### Traditional English translation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Salve_Regina&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Traditional English translation")]

> Hail, holy Queen, Mother of Mercy,  
> Hail our life, our sweetness and our hope.  
> To thee do we cry,  
> Poor banished children of Eve;  
> To thee do we send up our sighs,  
> Mourning and weeping in this valley (*or [vale](/wiki/Vale_of_tears "Vale of tears")*)[[a]](#cite_note-13) of tears.  
>   
> Turn then, most gracious advocate,  
> Thine eyes of mercy toward us;  
> And after this our exile,  
> Show unto us the blessed fruit of thy womb, Jesus.  
> O clement, O loving,  
> O sweet Virgin Mary.  
>   
> ℣ Pray for us, O holy Mother of God,  
> ℟ that we may be made worthy of the promises of Christ.  
>   
> Let us pray:  
> Almighty, everlasting God, who by the co-operation of the Holy Spirit didst prepare the body and soul of the glorious Virgin-Mother Mary to become a dwelling-place meet for thy Son: grant that as we rejoice in her commemoration; so by her fervent intercession we may be delivered from present evils and from everlasting death. Through the same Christ our Lord. Amen.

### Leonine version

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Salve_Regina&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Leonine version")]

The Salve Regina was one of the [Leonine Prayers](/wiki/Leonine_Prayers "Leonine Prayers"), in which context the [collect](/wiki/Collect "Collect") at the end was replaced by different text:

> *Oremus. Deus, refugium nostrum et virtus, populum ad te clamantem propitius respice; et intercedente gloriosa, et immaculata Virgine Dei Genitrice Maria, cum beato Joseph, ejus Sponso, ac beatis Apostolis tuis Petro et Paulo, et omnibus Sanctis, quas pro conversione peccatorum, pro libertate et exaltatione sanctae Matris Ecclesiae, preces effundimus, misericors et benignus exaudi. Per eundem Christum Dominum nostrum. Amen.*

> Let us pray. O God, our refuge and our strength, look down with mercy upon the people who cry to Thee; and by the intercession of the glorious and immaculate Virgin Mary, Mother of God, of Saint Joseph her spouse, of the blessed Apostles Peter and Paul, and of all the saints, in Thy mercy and goodness hear our prayers for the conversion of sinners, and for the liberty and exaltation of our Holy Mother the Church. Through the same Christ Our Lord. Amen.

### Modernized USCCB translation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Salve_Regina&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Modernized USCCB translation")]

The [United States Conference of Catholic Bishops](/wiki/United_States_Conference_of_Catholic_Bishops "United States Conference of Catholic Bishops") has a more modern translation which is used in the [ICEL](/wiki/ICEL "ICEL")'s translation of the [Liturgy of the Hours](/wiki/Liturgy_of_the_Hours "Liturgy of the Hours") and is used in the United States and Canada:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Sorrowful_Mother_Shrine_Chapel_%28Bellevue%2C_Ohio%29_-_narthex_mural%2C_Hail_Holy_Queen.jpg/250px-Sorrowful_Mother_Shrine_Chapel_%28Bellevue%2C_Ohio%29_-_narthex_mural%2C_Hail_Holy_Queen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sorrowful_Mother_Shrine_Chapel_(Bellevue,_Ohio)_-_narthex_mural,_Hail_Holy_Queen.jpg)

A painting of the prayer inside Sorrowful Mother Shrine Chapel ([Bellevue, Ohio](/wiki/Bellevue,_Ohio "Bellevue, Ohio"))

> Hail, holy Queen, mother of mercy,  
> Hail our life, our sweetness, and our hope.  
> To you we cry, poor banished children of Eve;  
> to you we send up our sighs,  
> mourning and weeping in this valley of tears.  
>   
> Turn, then, most gracious advocate,  
> your eyes of mercy toward us;  
> and after this, our exile,  
> show unto us the blessed fruit of your womb, Jesus.  
> O clement, O loving, O sweet Virgin Mary.  
>   
> ℣ Pray for us, O holy Mother of God.  
> ℟ That we may be made worthy of the promises of Christ.

[[13]](#cite_note-14)

A 1969 translation by James Quinn, beginning "Hail, our Queen and Mother blest,"[[14]](#cite_note-15) is offered as an alternative to the Latin in the non-ICEL translation of the Liturgy of the Hours (Divine Office) and is used in Australia, England and Wales, Ireland and Scotland.

## Indulgence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Salve_Regina&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Indulgence")]

[Pope Pius VI](/wiki/Pope_Pius_VI "Pope Pius VI") in the decree of 5 April 1786 granted the [indulgence](/wiki/Indulgence "Indulgence") of one hundred days and, on Sundays, of 7 years and as many as forty years to anyone with a contrite heart who recited the antiphon *Salve Regina* in the morning and the evening the *[Sub tuum praesidium](/wiki/Sub_tuum_praesidium "Sub tuum praesidium")*.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

This type of indulgences expressed in days or years was suppressed by the *[Indulgentiarum Doctrina](/wiki/Indulgentiarum_Doctrina "Indulgentiarum Doctrina")* of 1967.

The *[Enchiridion Indulgentiarum](/wiki/Enchiridion_Indulgentiarum "Enchiridion Indulgentiarum")* of 2004 provides for partial indulgence.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

## Hail, Holy Queen enthroned above

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Salve_Regina&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hail, Holy Queen enthroned above")]

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[Hail, holy Queen enthroned above](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:Hail,_holy_Queen_enthroned_above "s:en:Hail, holy Queen enthroned above")**

Modern version of the 1736 melody.

German priest [Johann Georg Seidenbusch](/wiki/Johann_Georg_Seidenbusch "Johann Georg Seidenbusch") published a hymn entitled "Gegrüßet seist du, Königin" in his 1687 devotional book *Marianischer Schnee-Berg*.[[17]](#cite_note-seidenbusch-18) This hymn was inspired by the pilgrimage devotions at [Aufhausen Priory](/wiki/Aufhausen_Priory "Aufhausen Priory"),[[17]](#cite_note-seidenbusch-18) and contains various salutations taken predominantly from the *Salve Regina*.

This hymn was soon to be found in various forms in many Catholic devotional books, and a Latin translation, "*Salve Regina coelitum*",[[18]](#cite_note-19) was soon created. The modern melody first appeared in the 1736 hymnal *Geistliche Spiel- und Weckuhr*, and Melchior Ludwig Herold's 1808 hymnal *Choralmelodien zum Heiligen Gesänge* contained the version that is standard today.

The English translation "Hail, Holy Queen enthroned above" first appeared in *The Roman Missal* in 1884.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

## Musical settings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Salve_Regina&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Musical settings")]

Main category: [Salve Regina settings](/wiki/Category:Salve_Regina_settings "Category:Salve Regina settings")

As an essential component of the Compline service, the hymn has been set to music by various composers, including [Victoria](/wiki/Tom%C3%A1s_Luis_de_Victoria "Tomás Luis de Victoria"), [Palestrina](/wiki/Giovanni_Pierluigi_da_Palestrina "Giovanni Pierluigi da Palestrina"), [Josquin](/wiki/Josquin_des_Prez "Josquin des Prez") and [Lassus](/wiki/Orlande_de_Lassus "Orlande de Lassus"). [Charpentier](/wiki/Marc-Antoine_Charpentier "Marc-Antoine Charpentier"), (5 settings, H.18, H.47, H.23, H.24, H.27), [Louis-Nicolas Clérambault](/wiki/Louis-Nicolas_Cl%C3%A9rambault "Louis-Nicolas Clérambault"), (C.114), [Giovanni Battista Pergolesi](/wiki/Giovanni_Battista_Pergolesi "Giovanni Battista Pergolesi") (3 settings), [Nicola Porpora](/wiki/Nicola_Porpora "Nicola Porpora") (3 settings), [Alessandro Scarlatti](/wiki/Alessandro_Scarlatti "Alessandro Scarlatti"), [Vivaldi](/wiki/Antonio_Vivaldi "Antonio Vivaldi"), [Jan Dismas Zelenka](/wiki/Jan_Dismas_Zelenka "Jan Dismas Zelenka"), (7 settings, ZWV 135-141, and ZWV 204), [Johann Adolph Hasse](/wiki/Johann_Adolf_Hasse "Johann Adolf Hasse") (3 settings), [Handel](/wiki/George_Frideric_Handel "George Frideric Handel"), and [Liszt](/wiki/Franz_Liszt "Franz Liszt") composed their own settings in later years. [Schubert](/wiki/Franz_Schubert "Franz Schubert") composed no fewer than seven versions. [Francis Poulenc](/wiki/Francis_Poulenc "Francis Poulenc") composed his setting of the text in 1941. [Arvo Pärt](/wiki/Arvo_P%C3%A4rt "Arvo Pärt") composed [a setting](/wiki/Salve_Regina_(P%C3%A4rt) "Salve Regina (Pärt)") first performed in the [Essen Cathedral](/wiki/Essen_Minster "Essen Minster") in 2002. [Olivier Latry](/wiki/Olivier_Latry "Olivier Latry") premiered in 2007 an organ work **[Salve Regina](/wiki/Salve_Regina_(Latry) "Salve Regina (Latry)")** which reflects in seven movements the lines of the hymn in [Gregorian chant](/wiki/Gregorian_chant "Gregorian chant").[[20]](#cite_note-21)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Salve_Regina&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

In 1976 the words of the first verse of the Salve Regina were used as a repeating theme in the song *[Oh What a Circus](/wiki/Oh_What_a_Circus "Oh What a Circus")* in the musical *[Evita](/wiki/Evita_(musical) "Evita (musical)")*, with music by [Andrew Lloyd Webber](/wiki/Andrew_Lloyd_Webber "Andrew Lloyd Webber") and lyrics by [Tim Rice](/wiki/Tim_Rice "Tim Rice").[[21]](#cite_note-22)

[Salve Regina University](/wiki/Salve_Regina_University "Salve Regina University"), a U.S. university established by the [Sisters of Mercy](/wiki/Sisters_of_Mercy "Sisters of Mercy") in 1934, was named in honor of the Salve Regina hymn and motto.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

The hymn is sung (using the English translation "Hail, Holy Queen enthroned above") by a choir of nuns in the 1992 comedy film *[Sister Act](/wiki/Sister_Act "Sister Act")*, starring [Whoopi Goldberg](/wiki/Whoopi_Goldberg "Whoopi Goldberg"). In the film, the hymn is initially sung in the traditional style, before shifting into an uptempo, [soul](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music") and [gospel music](/wiki/Gospel_music "Gospel music")–influenced arrangement. This arrangement has a bridge that intersperses lines from another [Marian hymn](/wiki/Hymns_to_Mary "Hymns to Mary"), *[O sanctissima](/wiki/O_sanctissima "O sanctissima")*, as well as the first lines of the *[Sanctus](/wiki/Sanctus "Sanctus")* (a prayer recited at [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)")).
